coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photocatalytic degradation of xanthate over carbon quantum dots embedded on BiOI nanosheets under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Zheng, Yufei
Zhang, Yuting
Wang, Yubo
Yuan, Fei
Gao, Dengzheng
Guo, Qingbin
Wang, Li
Hu, Xiaolong
coredata.dc:description: Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized and embedded onto BiOI nanosheets to form the CQDs/BiOI composite by hydrothermal method and applied to the degradation of xanthate for the first time. The effect of different mass ratios of CQDs and BiOI on the photocatalytic degradation of xanthate was investigated. Compared with pure BiOI, CQDs/BiOI composites exhibited enhanced photocatalytic activity for xanthate degradation under visible light. The highest xanthate degradation was up to 97.9 % at 5 wt% of CQDs to BiOI, and the reaction rate constant was about 12 times that of pure BiOI. The experimental and characterization results revealed the significantly enhanced photocatalytic performance was mainly attributed to the introduction of CQDs, which can greatly improve the segregation efficiency of photogenerated carriers through acting as the electron acceptors. The photocatalyst possessed good reusability for xanthate degradation indicating the great potential in practical application. In addition, the active radicals capturing experiments indicated that •O2 − were the main active species, and the possible photocatalytic mechanisms were also discussed in detail. This work provides an effective method to synthesize the promising visible-light-driven photocatalyst for wastewater treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
Carbon quantum dot
BiOI
Xanthate residue
Visible light

Shandong University of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by project 23-2-1-107-zyyd-jch supported by Qingdao Natural Science Foundation and Engineering Research Center of Non-metallic Minerals of Zhejiang Province ( ZD2023K03 ). The authors are grateful for the Shandong University of Science and Technology and all members of Professor Wang Li's research group for their support and help.

This work was supported by project 23-2-1-107-zyyd-jch supported by Qingdao Natural Science Foundation and Engineering Research Center of Non-metallic Minerals of Zhejiang Province (ZD2023K03). The authors are grateful for the Shandong University of Science and Technology and all members of Professor Wang Li's research group for their support and help.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: BiOI SEM images (a), 5 wt% CQDs/BiOI SEM images (b–c), 5 wt% CQDs/BiOI TEM images (d–f), and 5 wt% CQDs/BiOI EDS elemental maps (g–l).
XPS spectra of BiOI and 5 wt% CQDs/BiOI composite: (a) survey scan, (b) Bi 4f, (c) O 1s, (d) I 3d, (e) C 1s.
FT-IR spectra of CQDs/BiOI materials with different CQDs contents (a). Raman spectra of the samples (b).
UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (a), and band gap energies (b) of the samples.
Transient photocurrent response under visible light irradiation (a), and the electrochemical impedance spectra of the samples (b).

The degradation efficiency of different photocatalysts for xanthate under visible light irradiation (a), xanthate degradation kinetic curve (b), degradation efficiency under different light intencity (c), and reusability test of 5 wt% CQDs/BiOI (d).
Effect of different active species trapping agents on the photocatalytic degradation of xanthate by 5 wt% CQDs/BiOI.
Comparison of the degradation efficiency of catalysts used for photocatalytic degradation of xanthate in previous studies.

head.ce:title: Enhanced photocatalytic degradation of xanthate over carbon quantum dots embedded on BiOI nanosheets under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Energy and Mining Engineering, Shandong University of Science and Technology, Qingdao, 266590, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Energy and Mining Engineering
Shandong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Energy and Mining Engineering, Shandong University of Science and Technology, Qingdao, China, 266590

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Shandong University of Science and Technology, Qingdao, 266590, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Biological Engineering
Shandong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Shandong University of Science and Technology, Qingdao, China, 266590

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized and embedded onto BiOI nanosheets to form the CQDs/BiOI composite by hydrothermal method and applied to the degradation of xanthate for the first time. The effect of different mass ratios of CQDs and BiOI on the photocatalytic degradation of xanthate was investigated. Compared with pure BiOI, CQDs/BiOI composites exhibited enhanced photocatalytic activity for xanthate degradation under visible light. The highest xanthate degradation was up to 97.9 % at 5 wt% of CQDs to BiOI, and the reaction rate constant was about 12 times that of pure BiOI. The experimental and characterization results revealed the significantly enhanced photocatalytic performance was mainly attributed to the introduction of CQDs, which can greatly improve the segregation efficiency of photogenerated carriers through acting as the electron acceptors. The photocatalyst possessed good reusability for xanthate degradation indicating the great potential in practical application. In addition, the active radicals capturing experiments indicated that •O2 − were the main active species, and the possible photocatalytic mechanisms were also discussed in detail. This work provides an effective method to synthesize the promising visible-light-driven photocatalyst for wastewater treatment.

The dramatically improved CQDs/BiOI photocatalyst under visible light was synthesized vis hydrothermal method to effectively remove the xanthate wastewater. This work provides an effective method to synthesize the promising visible-light-driven photocatalyst for wastewater treatment.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: It is estimated that there are about 2 billion tons of minerals treated by flotation worldwide every year. Xanthate is a common trapping agent in the flotation process of sulfide ore, and the dosage is expected to reach 371.826 million tons by 2025 [1–3]. More than half of the xanthate are not consumed during the flotation process and are discharged into the environment, cause serious water pollution and ecological environment deterioration around the mine [4,5]. Therefore, treatment of mineral processing wastewater has become an important factor limiting the green development of the mining industry. At present, the approach and methodology of treating wastewater containing xanthate include adsorption, coagulation and precipitation, catalytic oxidation and biodegradation, etc [6–10]. Among them, photocatalysis is considered a promising green technology that can use semiconductor materials as photocatalysts to completely degrade hard-to-degrade pollutants into non-toxic or low-toxic small molecule substances [11–15]. Considering the efficient use of solar energy, the development of catalysts with visible light response are essential for practical use of photocatalysis research [16–19]. More and more researchers have started to explore new visible light-responsive photocatalysts, and after unremitting efforts, a series of highly progressive semiconductor photocatalysts such as CdS [20], g-C3N4 [21], Bi2WO6 [22], Bi2MoO6 [23] have been developed.

In recent years, BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I) has been proven to be the most prospective photocatalyst for the removal of organic pollutants and heavy metal pollutants [24–26]. Moreover, BiOI has a narrow band gap (1.8–2.0 eV), meaning it has a large optical absorption interval from the visible to the infrared spectral region [27]. And BiOI is a combination of interlaced [Bi2O2]2+ plates and double I plates, and this unique layered structure facilitates the migration and separation of photogenerated carriers due to the formation of an internal electric field [28,29]. However, the effectiveness of pure BiOI is still not satisfactory due to its high complexation rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs resulting from the hybridization of Bi6s orbitals and O2p orbitals [30,31]. Many researchers have proposed strategies to ameliorate the photocatalytic activity of BiOI, such as noble metal modification, heterojunction construction, crystal plane control, etc. A lot of modified semiconductor material catalyst such as BiOI/TiO2 [32,33], ZnO/BiOI [34], AgI/BiOI [35,36], ZnWO4/BiOI [37], MnOx−/BiOI [38], BiOCl/BiOI [39], g-C3N4/BiOI [40] and graphene/BiOI [41] have been developed under constant inquiry.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are widely used in various fields such as photocatalysis [42], biosensing [43] and chemiluminescence analysis [44] due to their simple preparation, stable luminescence, large specific surface area, high electron nimble, and good conductivity [45]. CQDs are a class of spherical particles less than 10 nm in diameter. Due to the conjugated π structure, CQDs exhibits admirably electron transfer and storage properties,and can be employed as co-catalysts to establish the surface separation center of photoinduced electrons, thereby obviouslyreducing the recombination rate of charge carriers [46]. This leads them to be promising for applications in photocatalysis. CQDs have now been introduced into several semiconductors, such as CQDs/g-C3N4 [47], CQDs/TiO2 [47], CQDs/BiOCl [48], CQDs/Bi2WO6 [49], and these photocatalysts presents the enhanced photocatalytic performance. However, most of the CQDs/semiconductor heterojunctions mentioned above have some obvious disadvantages, such as poor stability, difficulty in fabrication and high cost, which limit their practical application.

Considering the nature characteristics of CQDs and the limitations of BiOI, in this paper, CQDs/BiOI photocatalysts were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method to attach spherical CQDs on BiOI nanosheets, and applied to the xanthate degradation for the first time. The effect of mass ratios of CQDs and BiOI on photocatalytic degradation performance of xanthate was investigated. The enhanced photocatalytic degradation mechanisms of xanthate was proposed through the experimental and characterization results.

Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and KI (AR) were purchased from Xilong Technology Co. Natrium etilxanthate (C3H5NaOS2) was imported from Maclean Biochemical Technology Co. Ethanol (C2H6O) was introduced from Fuyu Fine Chemical Co. Benzoquinone (C6H4O2) from West Asia Chemical Industry Co. Methanol (CH4O) and Isopropanol (C3H8O) are from Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory. Urea (H2NCONH2) and Asam sitrat (C6H8O7) were obtained from Tianjin Dingsheng Xin Chemical Co., LTD and Qingdao Chemical Co., LTD.

Preparation of CQDs: under continuous agitation, 1 g urea and 3 g citrate were dissolved into 10 mL ultrapure water. The well-mixed solution was then transported to a teflon-lined stainless steel high-pressure reactor and kept at 180 °C for 5 h. The products were cooled naturally to room temperature and then centrifuged (10000 rpm, 30 min) to remove large particles. Subsequently, the CQDs powder were obtained after drying under vacuum at 60 °C.

Synthesis of CQDs/BiOCl catalysts: 1.5 g of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was added into 30 mL of deionized water, and constant stirring for 30 min at room temperature to taked shape a uniform white turbid liquid called solution A. Another 0.5 g of KI was added into the 20 mL of deionized water, then a certain amount of CQDs were evenly dispersed into the KI solution above after ultrasound for 15 min to form solution B. Under continuous stirring, solution B is added dropwise to solution A and react for 20min. After that, the mixture was poured into an high pressure reactor, and reacted at 160 °C for 12 h. Finally, the solidliquid mixture were centrifuged, washed with deionized water for some times and dried at 80 °C for 6 h. The CQDs/BiOI nanocomposites with different mass ratios of CQDs to BiOI were synthesized, which were denoted as 2 wt% CQDs/BiOI, 5 wt% CQDs/BiOI and 8 wt% CQDs/BiOI. The BiOI was produced by a similar method without the addition of CQDs.

The crystalline phases of the composites were analyzed by X-ray diffractometer (XRD). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) and energy disperse spectroscopy (EDX) were used to test the microstructure and distribution of each element. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to analyze the microstructure of the sample. An X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) calibrated for element C was used to analyze the elements of the sample. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Nicolet iS50 FT-IR) was used to measure the infrared spectrum and observe the chemical bonds and functional groups of the samples. Raman spectra are measured by Raman spectrometers. The optical properties of the samples were analyzed by UV–visible near infrared spectrophotometer and Hitachi F-4600 spectrometer. The CHI660e electrochemical workstation was used to determine the transient photocurrent (PC) and impedance (EIS) of the sample. The spectrophotometer (UV–vis) was used to determine the absorbance of the solution before and after the reaction at 301 nm wavelength, and the degradation efficiency of xanthate in a certain time was calculated.

The degradation efficiency of xanthate solution with different catalysts was evaluated. The experiment was operated in a photocatalytic reactor, and the xenon lamp with 420 nm filter (40 mW/cm2) was used to simulate the visible light sources. The volume of the reaction system was 50 mL, the concentration of xanthate was 50 mg/L, the amount of catalyst was 0.03g, and circulating cooling water was connected outside the reactor. After the colorimetric tube was placed into the photocatalytic apparatus, it was firstly stirred thoroughly and reacted under dark conditions for 60 min to reach the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Then turn on the light source and start the photocatalytic reaction. Take 3 mL of reaction solution at a certain interval, centrifuge and separate the supernatant. The absorbance of supernatant was measured by ultraviolet spectrophotometry at 300 nm, and the degradation efficiency of photocatalytic reaction was obtained.

The crystal structures of the prepared samples by XRD analysis are shown in Fig. 1 . The characteristic peaks at 2θ = 29.6°, 31.7°, 37.1°, 39.4°, 45.7°, 51.3°, 55.1°, and 66.1°correspond to the (102), (110), (103), (004), (104), (114), (212), and (220) facets of the orthorhombic phase BiOI, respectively, matching with the pure BiOI well. The XRD pattern of CQDs shows a broad peak centered at 26°, which is mainly due to the disordered carbon atoms [50]. The absence of characteristic peaks of CQDs in CQDs/BiOI composites is attributed to the low content and uniform distribution of CQDs in the composites, and the intensity of the BiOI diffraction peaks decreases as the content of CQDs increases. This result indicated that CQDs/BiOI composite catalyst was successfully synthesized.

SEM and TEM were used to investigate the morphology, structure and composition of the samples, and the results were shown in Fig. 2 . The SEM interpretation of result BiOI and 5 wt% CQDs/BiOI samples are shown in Fig. 2 (a) and Fig. 2 (b), respectively. The morphologies of the samples before and after compounding were flaky, showing that the addition of CQDs does not affect the morphological structure of BiOI. As can be seen in Fig. 2(d–f), CQDs with a size of about 5–6 nm are distributed on the BiOI nanosheets, indicating that BiOI is well compounded with CQDs. The 0.282 nm and 0.321 nm lattice stripes in Fig. 2 (f) are attributed to the (110) crystal plane of BiOI and the (002) crystal plane of CQDs, respectively [51]. The elements mapping patterns of Fig. 2(g–k) proves that the composite contains four elements Bi, O, I and C with uniform distribution. The EDS plots of the samples in Fig. 2 (l) reflect the characteristic peaks of the various elements and their corresponding atomic contents, which provides the another evidence for the successful formation of CQDs/BiOI composite.

In order to determine the elemental category and valence, XPS was used for the study on the samples, and the results are reflected in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3 (a) presents that the catalyst consists of Bi, O, I and C. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Bi 4f is shown in Fig. 3 (b). The peak with a binding energy of 165.3 eV in pure BiOI is attributed to Bi 4f
5/2 , indicating that the Bi in the sample is +3 valence [30]. The other peak at 159.9 eV is attributed to Bi 4f
7/2 . Compared with BiOI, the Bi 4f peak in 5 wt% CQDs/BiOI was shifted, indicating that the surface chemical state of Bi in the composite had been changed because of the contact between CQDs and BiOI [52]. As for O 1s in Fig. 3 (c), the peak at 531.5 eV belongs to the lattice oxygen in BiOI. Fig. 3 (d) shows the two crest value 632.4 eV and 620.8 eV are due to I 3d
3/2 and I 3d
5/2 , successively. Different from BiOI, the shift of I 3d peak for composite further indicates the presence of the coactions between CQDs and BiOI. Fig. 3 (e) shows the C 1s spectrum of composite, the crest value binding energies of 288.4 eV, 286.1 eV, and 284.9 eV are attributed to C–C bond, C–O bond, and CO group, successively [31]. Based on the XPS analysis results, the deposition of CQDs on BiOI surface to form CQDs/BiOI composite was confirmed.

FT-IR analysis was used for determining the surface functional groups in BiOI and CQDs/BiOI composites as shown in Fig. 4 (a). In all samples, the broad and strong peaks at around 3453 cm−1 and 1633 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of the O–H groups. The absorption bands located in the region 500-700 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibrations of Bi–O, Bi–O–I and Bi–O–Bi in bismuth oxyiodide [53]. The absorption band at 1382 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of CC, proving the existence of CQDs [54]. As the content of CQDs increases, the intensity of CC band also increases, indicating that CQDs are successfully introduced. The peak value at 613 cm−1 for all samples is because of the stretching vibration of Bi–O. The FT-IR results further reveal the successful formation of CQDs/BiOI composite. In addition, Raman spectroscopy is used for further investigating the surface structure of the material, and the test results are shown in Fig. 4 (b). Compared to pure BiOI, the 5 wt% CQDs/BiOI composite has a obvious peak at 1672 cm−1, which is put down to the presence of carbon in the composite [24,31]. The FT-IR and Raman results showed that CQDs were successfully attached to the surface of BOI nanosheets.

The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra were measured as shown in Fig. 5 to determine the band gap and light absorption capacity of the samples. As seen from Fig. 5 (a), the light absorption of pure BiOI ranges from the UV to the visible at 670 nm, indicating the significant absorption ability of BiOI in the visible region. The steep-edged absorption bands of all samples in the visible region suggests that the absorption of visible light is due to a jump in the band gap rather than the energy levels of other impurities [55]. It also indicates that the introduction of CQDs serves as a modifier rather than an impurity atom. In addition, the CQDs/BiOI composites exhibit much stronger light absorption compared to pure BiOI, revealing that CQDs can extend the visible light response range. Fig. 5 (b) shows the band gap values calculated by the classical Tauc formula. It can be seen that the introduction of CQDs reduces the band gap value of BiOI, which promotes the electron leap and thus being beneficial for the improvement of photocatalytic activity [56].

Photocatalytic reactivity of the material is closely related to the separating efficiency of photogenerated hole-electron pairs, and high complex efficiency results in the poor photocatalytic activity. The stronger the intensity of the glow, the lower the separation efficiency of the carriers involved in photocatalysis [41]. As can be seen from Fig. 6 , compared with BiOI, the photoluminescence intensity of the composites decreases to different degrees, indicating that the addition of CQDs can effectively improve the separating efficiency of photogenerated carriers. And this effect is connected with the additive amount of CQDs, when the content of CQDs is 5 %, the hightest separation efficiency is obtained.

It is well known that charge separation is the most critical factor in determining photocatalytic efficiency [57], and photocurrent testing is another way to assess the separation efficiency of photogenerated hole-electron pairs. As shown in Fig. 7 (a), the photocurrent response of all composites was found to be higher than that of BiOI, manifesting that the addition of CQDs improved the charge-transfer efficiency. As the content of CQDs increased, the intensity of photocurrent response increased first and then decreased, and 5 wt% CQDs/BiOI composite showed the highest separation efficiency of photoexcited carriers.

To further investigate the transfer ability of photogenerated electrons in samples, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used and the results were shown in Fig. 7 (b). The EIS radii of the CQDs/BiOI composites were much smaller than that of pure BiOI, and 5 wt% CQDs/BiOI had the smallest radius among samples. The smaller the radius of the EIS curve was, the faster the charge transferred and the more high charge separation efficiency [58]. The analysis results demonstrated that the addition of CQDs could obviously improve the transfer and separation efficiency of photoinduced carriers, thereby promoting the enhancement of photocatalytic activity.

The photocatalytic activity of CQDs/BiOI synthetic material and BiOI was appraised by comparing the degradation efficiency of the xanthate solution under visible light irradiation, and the results are shown in Fig. 8 . As shown in Fig. 8 (a), a dark adsorption reaction was performed for 60 min before the photocatalytic reaction to erase the effect of adsorption on the photocatalytic reaction. The results of the dark adsorption reaction showed that the adsorption-resolution equilibrium was reached at 30 min of dark reaction. It can be seen that within 30 min, the adsorption of BiOI, 2 wt% CQDs/BiOI, 5 wt% CQDs/BiOI, 8 wt% CQDs/BiOI, and 11 wt% CQDs/BiOI were 17.1 %, 37.2 %, 53.7 %, 44.7 %, and 24.8 %, respectively. In terms of photocatalytic activity, BiOI exhibited poor photocatalytic degradation efficiency (33.3 %) for xanthate solution after 75 min of light exposure, and this is mainly attributed to the higher recombination rate of hole-electron pairs in the photocatalytic process [29]. In addition, the content of CQDs had a obvious influence on the photocatalytic activity. The xanthate degradation efficiency of 2 wt% CQDs/BiOI, 5 wt% CQDs/BiOI, 8 wt% CQDs/BiOI, and 11 wt% CQDs/BiOI composites were 90.9 %, 97.8 %, 95.1 %, and 56.2 %, respectively. The presence of a conjugate structure in CQDs would act as the electron acceptors to transfer electrons and inhibit the recombination of carriers [50]. As the content of CQDs increased, the degradation first increased and then decreased. When a small amount of CQDs was introduced into BiOI, CQDs could act as an acceptor for electron transfer under visible light irradiation and improve the separation efficiency of carriers, thus improving the photocatalytic degradation performance of CQDs/BiOI materials. As the content of CQDs increased, CQDs can absorb a portion of the visible light, and when the content of CQDs exceeds 5 %, excessive CQDs on BiOI nanosheets will hinder its absorption of visible light. In general, semiconductor materials need to absorb light to produce hole electron pairs, and then which participate in redox reactions. The decrease in light absorption would affect the production of hole electron pairs, causing the decrease of photocatalytic activity [59]. Besides, the ptotcatalytic reaction conformed to a pseudo-first order kinetic model as seen in Fig. 8 (b). The reaction rate constants of BiOI, 2 wt% CQDs/BiOI, 5 wt% CQDs/BiOI, 8 wt% CQDs/BiOI and 11 wt% CQDs/BiOI were 0.0051, 0.0305, 0.0611, 0.0405, 0.0102, respectively. The 5 wt% CQDs/BiOI material had largest reaction rate constant, which was about 12 times higher than that of BiOI, and this is mainly ascribed to the appropriate mass ratio of CQDs and BiOI in this composite leading to higher light response ability and carriers’ separation efficiency. In addition, the quantum efficiency (QE) of samples were also calculated according to the following formula: QE=(j
ph × hc/e)/(P
mono
×λ)= (j ph × 1239.8)/(P
mono
×λ). Where jph is the photocurrent density (mA/cm2), h is the planck constant (6.62 × 10⁻34 J s), c is light velocity, e is quantity of electricity for single electron, P
mono is light power density of monochromatic light and λ is the wavelength of monochromatic light. In this study, P
mono is 40 mW/cm2 and λ is 380 nm. The jphs of BiOI, 2 wt% CQDs/BiOI, 5 wt% CQDs/BiOI and 8 wt% CQDs/BiOI are 0.0215, 0.0235, 0.0260 and 0.0245 mA/cm2, respectively, and so the corresponding QE are 0.15 %, 0.17 %, 0.20 % and 0.18 %, respectively. It is clear that 5 wt% CQDs/BiOI possesses the good light energy utilization efficiency, and which would contribute to the enhancement of the photocatalytic activity.

Furthermore, the effect of light intensity on the xanthate degradation was investigated as shown in Fig. 8 (c). The xanthate degradation efficiency exhibits an obvious enhancement from 85.6 % to 97.8 % when the light intensity increases from 30 mW/cm2 to 40 mW/cm2. When the light intensity is up to 50 mW/cm2, the xanthate degradation is about 99.5 %. This is because under high light intensity, more electron hole pairs can be formed, thereby producing more active substances to degrade the xanthate. Besides, the xanthate are almost completely degraded at light intensity of 40 mW/cm2, which is also the reason why this light intensity was chosen for the experiment in this study. In addition, the reusability of photocatalysts is another significative factor in the pratical application of photocatalytic materials. To prove the reusability of the 5 wt% CQDs/BiOI material, five photocatalytic cycle tests were performed under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 8 (d), the degradation efficiency can still reach 79.3 % after five cycles, which indicated that the prepared photocatalysts have good reusability. In addition, in contrast with the previous studies, the photocatalytic xanthate degradation activity of 5 wt% CQDs/BiOI in this study is superior to most reported photocatalysts as listed in Table 1 . Notably, this study provides an effective method for photocatalytic degradation of xanthate wastewater.

To detect the active species that play a crucial part in the photocatalytic process, free radicals trapping experiments were performed. Methanol, isopropanol, and p-benzoquinone are used as the scavengers of h+, •OH, and •O2 −, respectively [64–66]. As seen in Fig. 9 , after adding the methanol, isopropanol, and p-benzoquinone, the corresponding degradation rates of xanthate under the same conditions were 78.3 %, 85.1 % and 51.7 %, and the inhibition rates were 19.6 %, 12.8 % and 46.2 %, in sequence. The results indicated that the photocatalytic effect did not decrease significantly after the addition of isopropanol and methanol, indicating that •OH and h+ did not play a crucial part in the reaction process. In contrast, the photocatalytic effect decreased significantly after the addition of p-benzoquinone, indicating that •O2 − in the reaction process.

According to the above information discussed results, a photocatalytic reaction mechanism diagram of CQDs/BiOI nanocomposites was proposed as illustrated in Fig. 10 . BiOI has a forbidden band value of 1.72 eV and is easily excited by visible light, where electrons are transferred from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band(CB), leaving holes in CB. The EVB and ECB of BiOI calculated by the formula:
E VB
= X ‐ E
e
+ 0.5 E
g
E CB
= E
VB
‐ E
g
X represents the electronegativity of the semiconductor (5.99 eV), Ee represents the energy of the free electron (4.5 eV), and Eg represents the forbidden band gap of the semiconductor (1.72 eV). The EVB and ECB of BiOI were 2.35 eV and 0.63 eV, respectively. Since the potential of its VB is higher than •OH/OH−, h+ in VB can react directly with water to form •OH [53]. In addition, as CQDs possess conjugate systems, they are good electron acceptors and transferors, which can achieve the separation of photogenerated carriers. The electrons in CB transfer to the surface of CQDs and react with O2 to produce •O2 −, thus suppressing the reconfiguration probability of hole-electron pairs and prolonging the lifetime of charge carriers. Then, these active species produced during the whole reaction, such as h+, •OH and •O2 −, would gradually degrade the xanthate molecules into other intermediate products or H2O and CO2. Therefore, BiOI modified with CQDs can improve the photocatalytic performance by improving the detach efficiency of hole electron pairs. This study provides an effective scheme for the efficient degradation and removal of xanthate in flotation wastewater.

In the present study, CQDs/BiOI nanocomposites were produced by hydrothermal method, which can effectively remove xanthate from flotation processing wastewater. Experimental data showed that the photocatalytic effect was significantly improved after the addition of CQDs. The degradation efficiency of 5 wt% CQDs/BiOI could reach up to 97.9 % within 75 min, and the reaction rate constant of 0.06106 was about 12 times that of pure BiOI. And the active radicals trapping experimental results indicated that •O2 − radicals were the main active substances in the xanthate degradation process. In addition, the cycling test results showed that the xanthate degradation was still as high as 79.3 % after 5 cycles, indicating that 5 wt% CQDs/BiOI photocatalyst had a good reusability. In this study, carbon quantum dots were successfully introduced as acceptors for electron transfer to ameliorate the detach efficiency of hole electron pairs and enhance the degradation efficiency of xanthate in mineral processing wastewater. Furthermore, CQDs/BiOI nanocomposites were produced by a simple hydrothermal method, and the use of water as the solvent can greatly reduce the fabrication cost. The preparation of photocatalyst provides an effective way for photocatalytic degradation of xanthate.

Yufei Zheng: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Yuting Zhang: Investigation. Yubo Wang: Investigation. Fei Yuan: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Dengzheng Gao: Validation, Visualization. Qingbin Guo: Validation, Visualization. Li Wang: Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Xiaolong Hu: Project administration, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by project 23-2-1-107-zyyd-jch supported by Qingdao Natural Science Foundation and Engineering Research Center of Non-metallic Minerals of Zhejiang Province (ZD2023K03). The authors are grateful for the Shandong University of Science and Technology and all members of Professor Wang Li's research group for their support and help.
